segments.s188-g17: نبینم is first-person negative present/past form meaning “I do not see / I see not.” ز here is the poetic preposition meaning “from/among.” خودکامه is an adjective ‘self-willed, autocratic’ modifying the following plural proper name گودرزیان (the Gudarzis). The sequence places the verb first for emphasis, then the prepositional phrase describing whose company is being referred to.
segments.s188-g18: مگر is used as an exception marker ‘except / unless.’ آنک is a relative pronoun ‘the one who / which.’ دارد is third‑person singular present/past auxiliary/main verb here meaning ‘has / brings’ (i.e., causes). سپه را زیان is the object: سپه ‘army/host’ marked by را (definite object marker) and زیان ‘harm / loss,’ so together ‘who brings harm to the army.’
segments.s188-g19: بترسیدی is second-person past tense ‘you feared / you were afraid.’ از is the preposition ‘of / from.’ بی‌هنر is the adjective ‘unskilled’ modifying یک سوار ‘one rider / a single horseman.’ Word order places the verb up front followed by the prepositional phrase and its noun.
segments.s188-g20: نه negates the following nominal clause (‘not’). شیر ژیان is a compound where شیر is ‘lion’ and ژیان here conveys the sense ‘life / spiritedness,’ producing the idiom ‘lion-like’ or a brave, heroic man. بود is the past copula ‘was.’ بر is the preposition ‘on/upon,’ and کوهسار is ‘mountain/highland.’ The clause means ‘there was no lion-like man on the mountain.’
segments.s188-g21: سپه is the subject ‘the army/host.’ دید is the past tense ‘saw / beheld.’ و is the conjunction ‘and.’ برگشت is past ‘returned / turned (back).’ سوی is the preposition ‘toward,’ and فریب is a noun meaning ‘deception / treachery,’ so the clause reads ‘the army saw and turned toward deception.’
segments.s188-g22: بخیره is an adverbial phrase ‘for safety / safely’ modifying the verb سپردی, which is second-person past ‘you entrusted / handed over.’ فراز و نشیب is a paired nominal object ‘the heights and the descents’ (ups and downs), joined by و, and is what was entrusted. Word order places the adverb before the verb and the coordinated object after it.
segments.s188-g9: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/so” placed front for emphasis. داد here is the past-tense verb “gave” used with پاسَخ (answer) as its object; the phrase is literally “thus gave (an) answer.” ستمکاره is an epithet “the oppressor” attached to طوس (Tūs) after the noun, a common poetic apposition.
segments.s188-g10: که introduces the reported content (’that’ / introducing the speech). من is the emphatic first‑person subject, and دارم is the present possessive verb “I have.” این + a list (لشکر و بوق و کوس) enumerates the possessions: “this army and horn and drum.” Word order reflects a poetic, somewhat loose ordering but reads as a straightforward declarative clause.
segments.s188-g11: ترا (t + را) is the old-fashioned object form “you” placed before the verb (’I told you’). گفتم is the past verb “I said/told” (here functioning as ‘I told you’). او را is the object “him” with the object marker را; بنزد + من is a prepositional phrase meaning “to my presence/near me.” آر is an imperative verb meaning “bring” (archaic/poetic infinitive/imperative); the printed senses list does not show this verbal sense, but context requires an imperative meaning.
segments.s188-g12: سخن (speech/talk) is the object/topic placed up front. هیچگونه is a negating modifier “in no way/of no kind.” مکن is the imperative negative “do not (do).” خواستار here refers to being a petitioner/seeker; together the phrase counsels ‘do not petition/ask for any speech’ or more simply ‘do not ask for any talk.’
segments.s188-g13: گر is the conditional ‘if’. او is the third‑person pronoun ‘he’. شهریارست is a poetic contraction meaning ‘is king’ (شهریار + است → شهریارست). پس is the resultative ‘then’. من کیم is an archaic interrogative order literally ‘I who am?’ = ‘who am I?’
segments.s188-g14: برین is بر + این ‘to/on this’ (poetic fusion) modifying کوه ‘mountain’. گوید is the present/factual verb ‘says’ (here rhetorical). ز بهر is a compound prepositional phrase ‘for the sake of / on account of’. چیم is an archaism meaning ‘what of me?/for me’ used in a rhetorical question ‘what will he say/do on my account?’
segments.s188-g15: یکی is the indefinite ‘a certain / one’. ترک‌زاده is an epithet ‘Turk-born / of Turkic descent’. چو is the simile ‘like/as’. زاغ سیاه is the simile’s image ‘black crow/rook’ (a negative or ominous comparison). The whole phrase introduces a person by comparison.
segments.s188-g16: برین گونه is a prepositional + demonstrative + noun phrase ‘in this manner / in this way’ (بر = to/toward, ین = variant of این, گونه = manner). بگرفت is the past verb ‘took / seized / seized upon’ (ب‑ + گرفت). راه سپاه is the noun phrase ‘the army’s road/route’ (راه = road/path, سپاه = army). The verb governs the following object phrase.
segments.s188-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause. بهرام (proper name) is the subject, برگشت is the past-tense verb 'returned'. با طوس 'with Tous' is a prepositional phrase placed after the verb (common in Persian poetry). گفت is the past tense 'said/spoke' and leads into reported speech introduced by که in the next segment.
segments.s188-g2: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the content of what was said. با جان پاکت is a dative/with-phrase literally 'with your pure soul' (پاکت = thy purity, an archaic/poetic 2nd‑person possessive form). خرد باد is a fixed optative formula: خرد (here 'wisdom') + باد (the optative particle 'may'), and جفت ('pair'/'be paired') qualifies خرد: 'may wisdom be paired.' Word order (noun + باد + modifier) is typical of classical Persian optative expressions.
segments.s188-g3: بدان here functions as a demonstrative/locative compound (بـ- + دان) with the force 'to/unto that' (an archaic/poetic form). کان is an archaic demonstrative/particle tied to بدان; together they point to 'that one'. فرودست is an adjective 'lowly, subordinate', and فرزند شاه is a genitive phrase 'son of the king' (noun + possessor follows), so the whole segment names 'that low-born son of the king.'
segments.s188-g4: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. شد + کشته is a periphrastic passive (شد 'became/was' + past participle کشته) meaning 'was killed'. بر + بی‌گناه indicates the circumstance or moral relation 'upon/against (though) innocent' — i.e. 'was slain while innocent' or 'was slain unjustly.'
segments.s188-g5: نمود here is a past-tense verb 'showed / displayed' (an older poetic past form). آن نشانی is the direct object 'that sign/mark'. که introduces a relative clause 'which' and اندر نژاد is a prepositional phrase 'in/within (their) lineage' (اندر is a poetic preposition 'in', نژاد 'lineage/race'), linking the sign to descent.
segments.s188-g6: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. کاوس and کیقباد are proper names (Kay Kavus and Kay Qobad). دارند is the present-tense verb 'they have/possess' (here 'they bear/possess in their lineage'). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. The clause states the provenance of the lineage: 'from Kay Kavus and from Kay Qobad.'
segments.s188-g7: ترا (here split as ت + را in the wordlist) is the object 'to you' (ت = 2nd-person object, را = object/definiteness marker). شاه کیخسرو is the nominative subject 'King Kay Khosrow' (title + name). اندرز کرد is the past-tense verb phrase 'gave advice / warned' (اندرز 'advice' + کرد 'did/made' = 'gave counsel'). The order places the indirect object before subject, which is common in poetry for emphasis.
segments.s188-g8: که introduces reported content of the advice. گرد فرود here is a nominal phrase: گرد 'assembly/host/round' (poetic sense 'host' or 'assembled force') + فرود 'sent down/assembled against' (nounal or adjectival use), followed by سیاوش 'Siavash' as the target. مگرد is an archaic negative imperative ('do not become/join' or 'do not go'), so the clause means 'do not join the host sent against Siavash.' (Note: مگرد is an older poetic negative form.)

sentences.s188-s9: I see none among the self-willed Gudarzis, except the one who would bring harm to the army.
sentences.s188-s10: You were afraid of a single unskilled horseman; there was not a lion-like (brave) man upon the mountain.
sentences.s188-s11: The army saw and turned toward deceit; for safety you entrusted the heights and the lowlands.
sentences.s188-s5: Tūs the oppressor replied thus: “I have this army and horn and drum.”
sentences.s188-s6: I told you: bring him to my presence; do not ask for any speech.
sentences.s188-s7: If he is king, then who am I? — what does he say to this mountain on my account?
sentences.s188-s8: A certain Turk-born man, like a black crow, thus took the army’s road.
sentences.s188-s1: When Bahram returned he spoke with Tous, saying: “May wisdom be paired with your pure soul.”
sentences.s188-s2: To that lowly-born son of the king, Siavash — who was slain though innocent.
sentences.s188-s3: He showed that sign which they have in their lineage from Kay Kavus and from Kay Qobad.
sentences.s188-s4: King Kay Khosrow advised you: do not join the host assembled against Siavash.
